text:
During the fall of 2010, we conducted simultaneous measurements of particle hygroscopic growth (at 90% RH), cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity, and size-resolved chemical composition in the Thüringer Wald mountain range of central Germany. As particle size increased from 50 to 250 nm, the median hygroscopicity parameter ($\kappa$) derived from HTDMA measurements rose from 0.14 to 0.28, reflecting a clear size-dependent chemical shift.

We performed a closure analysis between HTDMA-measured hygroscopicity ($\kappa_{HTDMA}$) and values derived from chemical composition ($\kappa_{chem}$) using the Zdanovskii–Stokes–Robinson (ZSR) mixing rule. Our results show that relying on size-averaged chemical data leads to a significant overprediction of hygroscopicity—by as much as 30% to 40% for 150 and 100 nm particles, respectively. However, incorporating size-resolved composition substantially improved the agreement. We also identified that the evaporation of semivolatile $NH_4NO_3$ within the HTDMA system likely contributes to the remaining discrepancies between predicted and observed growth.

Furthermore, we found that the hygroscopicity of the organic fraction ($\kappa_{org}$) correlates positively with the oxygen-to-carbon (O:C) ratio ($\kappa_{org} = 0.19 \times [O:C] - 0.03$), providing a reliable way to define organic water uptake in closure models. Interestingly, $\kappa$ values derived from CCN measurements were consistently about 30% higher than those determined from sub-saturated HTDMA measurements. This difference suggests that factors such as surface tension reduction, solution non-ideality, and the co-condensation of semivolatile compounds play a more prominent role during activation. Consequently, we conclude that while $\kappa_{CCN}$ can be accurately predicted using size-resolved chemistry and the ZSR rule, caution must be exercised when extrapolating sub-saturated hygroscopicity data to predict cloud droplet activation.
